role_summary: Lawyer and speechwriter for Martin Luther King Jr.; helped draft "I Have a Dream" and circulated "Letter from Birmingham Jail"; first African-American allied member of the New York Stock Exchange (1967).
ai_reading: Jones sits at +0.67 on the Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense axis, distinctly toward the Armed Self-Defense pole. This is the most striking AI-vs-culture mismatch in the catalog so far: Jones is Martin Luther King Jr.'s personal counsel, the lawyer who drafted the opening of "I Have a Dream" at his Riverdale home and who carried the handwritten "Letter from Birmingham Jail" pages out of jail in April 1963. The cultural record places him squarely inside King's nonviolent theological apparatus. The embedding does not. The mismatch is sharper still given that on the same axis the Black Panther-identified interviewees in this corpus (Aaron Dixon at -0.73, Elmer Dixon at -1.00, Elbert Howard at -0.85, Kathleen Cleaver at -0.80) cluster at the OPPOSITE pole. Either the axis is reading something other than its label captures, or the embedding is finding Jones's legal-strategic vocabulary (movement-as-political-power, copyright-preservation, NYSE-allied-member) as closer to the self-defense vocabulary than to the homiletic nonviolence King's public speeches deployed.

photo.alt: Clarence B. Jones at the San Francisco International Film Festival, 2026

biographical_paragraph: When Martin Luther King Jr. wrote his "Letter from Birmingham Jail" in April 1963, the pages of handwriting were brought out of the jail by Clarence B. Jones, who arranged to have the text typed and circulated among clergy before the letter reached national print [src: 2]. Jones, born January 8, 1931 in Philadelphia, served as one of King's lawyers and speechwriters, and the two were close friends [src: 2]. The opening of King's August 1963 "I Have a Dream" speech was drafted at Jones's home in Riverdale, the Bronx; Jones also preserved King's copyright on the finished address [src: 2]. He contributed to King's April 4, 1967 "Beyond Vietnam" speech alongside Vincent Harding and Andrew Young [src: 2]. Jones's legal career ran in parallel to the movement work: he took a Columbia College Bachelor's in 1953 and a Boston University law degree in 1959, served as the first general counsel of the Gandhi Society for Human Rights in 1962, and in 1967 became the first African-American allied member of the New York Stock Exchange, joining the firm Carter, Berlind and Weill [src: 2]. He received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2024 [src: 2]. He died May 22, 2026 in Cupertino, California, at ninety-five [src: 2]; his Library of Congress oral history is entry 20 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

sources.title: Clarence B. Jones oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress

sources.title: Clarence B. Jones with Stuart Connelly, Behind the Dream: The Making of the Speech That Transformed a Nation
